dialogue.utterance: Units 3 and 4, respond to unconscious 54yo female in bathroom-[CITY], Apt 305.
Hi, I'm Alex with Rescue 3-what prompted the 911 call today?
She was found face down, unresponsive, with gasping breaths. I believe she was recently in the hospital for a UTI.
She does not respond to voice, pain, or touch. No attempt at movement or opening eyes.
No eye opening. No spontaneous movement or verbalization-GCS is 3 per initial assessment.
No verbalization-no response regardless of loud voice or pressure to extremities.
Free limb movement testing hindered by unresponsiveness and supine, central positioning. GCS motor response is 1 as well.
Airway is partially compromised-loud snoring sounds during breathing. NPA being prepared now.
Assist the partner-26F NPA ready to be placed to open the airway during assisted breaths.
Initial respiratory rate is 7 per minute-agonal and irregular. She's working hard, with visible accessory muscle use and paradoxical effort in breathing.
Very shallow depth with irregular pattern-airway non-patent contributing to breathing difficulties.
Agonal respirations confirmed-lots of effort with minimal air movement.
Bilateral breath sounds are clear, equal, and normal-no wheezes, crackles or rhonchi audible now.
Pulse ox is reading 0. Got a faulty cable-[VEHICLE_IDENTIFIER] is en route with replacement equipment.
Radial pulses-strong, equal, and regular. Radial pulses bounding on bilateral sides, with no signs of bradycardia or arrhythmia to this point.
First pulse measurement is 98-no bradycardia yet.
Skin warm, dry, and normal in color. No signs of cyanosis or mottling as of now.
Bilateral radial pulses strong and equal. Limbs show no sign of acute peripheral compromise.
Blood pressure initially 219 over 111-this is severely hypertensive. Second reading is 213 over 101.
Allergies reported? I see no evidence of known drug allergies, but she was recently inpatient-treated for a UTI and prescribed naloxone with no opioids found on scene today.
No known allergies. Her discharge from the inpatient stay several days ago included naloxone but no opioid prescriptions observed on arrival by us.

Prescribed medications include Docusate, Senna, Gabapentin, Mirtazapine, Metformin, Atorvastatin, Lisinopril, Oxybutynin, Furosemide. No controlled medications observed on scene or in patient belongings here today in her apartment.

She has a history of hyperlipidemia, primary hypertension, T2DM, chronic pain, and post CVA-related neurological deficits based on discharge notes and provided patient chart today from UTI treatment episode.
You discovered her in the bathroom alone, correct?
Yes. I don't understand why she's like this. She told me she was discharged and had been taking everything that was prescribed to her. She's very compliant-had a UTI, was getting better until now.
No pain presented or described by patient-algometry testing will be inconclusive due to unresponsiveness when assessed at scene initially.
Skin is warm, dry-capillary refill <2 seconds. No signs of pallor or jaundice.
We were able to complete GCS and eye exams before naloxone, but patient remains conscious but a/ox3/4-limited memory and orientation to place and purpose.
She said she was walking in the house when she lost consciousness-now she's confused and scared but denies knowing where she is.
Pupils are PERRL-3mm in size, equal, reactive to light, and tracking normally. No damage seen to eyes or trauma to nose or face detected on visual examination.
No signs of jugular venous distention, swelling, or tenderness detected on neck exam before naloxone administration which caused patient agitation.
Chest shows regular rise and fall on exam and BVM support. Bilateral breath sounds remain equal, clear, and symmetrical-no signs of pneumothorax or tension chest.
No deformities, abrasions, contusions, punctures, or burns visible on torso, chest, or abdomen-directed by BVM mask and NPA placement.
No swelling, cuts, masses, or deformities to arms or legs on visual field assessment. Equal movement attempted bilaterally. Track marks noted on left AC area.
Motor and sensory strength found to be present initially-post-naloxone resistance limited further testing.
Both feet and hands examined-found with full range of motion, no crepitus or instability. Skin color and warmth appeared normal before combative behavior emerged.
PMS tests confirmed-positive motor responses bilaterally, but attempts to re-test disrupted by patient's combative behavior.
Post-naloxone, heart rate found again at 93 BPM-relatively stable.
Post-naloxone respiratory count appears regular and increased to 14-significant improvement observed from initial agonal breathing.
BP post-naloxone was recorded at 213/101-still elevated, but appearance of hemodynamic instability has not changed.
Measured initial glucose at 600-elevated. No follow-up readings due to repeated patient disruption and removal of monitoring devices.
Post replacement of cable, spO2 measured at 100 after naloxone.
EKG remains in sinus rhythm with no significant ST deviations reported in initial and subsequent assessments.
Per protocol 3-15-performing 4-lead ECG monitoring to identify any cardiac causes of this event.
Capnography initiated to verify BVM-assisted ventilation and monitor for acidosis.
Inserted 26F NPA to assist with airway management given snoring and non-patent status initially.
Administering 15LPM BVM ventilation via mask to maintain gas exchange pending further response.
Providing 4LPM oxygen via nasal cannula initially-post-naloxone, switched to flow-by for supplemental air.
Naloxone being prepped-1.0mg IM to the left deltoid in case of opioid-induced impairment, given her naloxone prescription and recent hospital stay indicating history of such concerns.
Re-exam: Bilateral breath sounds still normal and symmetric-no new findings in airway condition post-BVM assisted ventilation.
She is awake but confused, only oriented to self. Significant window exists for delirium, hypoglycemia, hyperglycemia, or intrinsic neurological deficits.
Confused and combative-GCS elevated but limited interaction ability. Friends identify her correctly but she is agitated and difficult to console.
She's now reacting violently but transport needs prioritizing. Agitated behavior a risk for crew safety and medical integrity on scene.
Applying restraints with shoulder and lap belts for stabilizing patient on stretcher-decreasing possibility of self-harm or resistance during transport.
Hospital is [COMPANY_NAME]-Room 20. Alerted and current with pre-report before departure.
Arrival at [COMPANY_NAME]-Cold Room 20-who is ready for patient hand-off?
Patient secured in ambulance-nestled properly and bariatric sling not required. Ambulance staff coordinating route and restraints.
Again checking glucose levels as a result of altered mental status. Final reading remains 600 from initial staging.
Post-naloxone spO2 was 100-remained stable under BVM transport conditions.
Transport to [COMPANY_NAME] Cold Room 20 is stable and compliant with protocol 3-15. Consciousness intermittent due to behavioral disruptions during movement.
ED Room 20 is ready to receive-Vitals, medications, and results from capnography and EKG provided on arrival.
Post-naloxone, BVM and NPA no longer required due to improved airway patency.
Should I attempt IV line placement at this time given changes in recumbent position post-restraining and patient's ongoing agitation?
No pain reported during her current lucid but combative states. Pain is not the presenting issue today.
Chief complaint still altered mental status-history of hyperglycemia, HTN, and stroke with significant medical comorbidities to support this assessment.
Rechecking vitals-blood pressure remains 213/101, respiratory rate 14, pulse 93. EKG sinus rhythm has remained consistent.
Last check-ambulance secure. Patient remains in restraints but no further interventions needed on board.
Transfer of care complete. [COMPANY_NAME] staff have patient and all field assessments and procedural documentation.
Ambulance cleared and on standby. Redirecting to next call.
